events.title: Button Book Club
events.description: Do you love to read but hate deadlines? Or are you just too busy to attend traditional book club meetings? If so, our Button Book Club is perfect for you! Each month there will be a new book to read and a brand new pin to collect! How It Works Pick Your Book! Each month will feature a new Easy/Picture, Beginning Reader, Middle Grade, and Teen book. Simply pick which level you want to read and check out the book! Share Your Thoughts! Come back anytime during the month and write/draw your favorite part of the book. Get Your Pin! Each book will have it's own special, collectible pin. Participate every month to collect them all! Plus, get a free lanyard with your first pin to show them off! April Books: Easy/Picture: Exactly as Planned by Tao Nyeu Beginning Readers: Mirror Magic by Linda Chapman Middle Grade: The Cookie Crumbles by Tracy Badua Teen: Costumes for Time Travelers by A.R. Capetta

events.title: Reading Bingo
events.description: Join us in celebrating Día del Niño (Day of the Child) at the Union City Library throughout April. Pick up a "Reading Bingo" card at the reference desk and complete five reading activities across or diagonally on your own or with a reading friend. Show us your completed bingo card at the reference desk by Saturday, May 2, 2026, for a small prize, while supplies last. Día del Niño (Day of the Child) is celebrated in Mexico every year on April 30. It is a holiday that highlights the wonder and joy children bring into our lives. The library has adapted Día del Niño; we celebrate Día del Niño & Libro by promoting reading and children’s literacy. Acompáñanos a celebrar el Día del Niño en la Biblioteca de Union City durante todo el mes de abril. Recoge tu tarjeta de "Reading Bingo" en el mostrador de consulta y completa cinco actividades de lectura tú solo o con un compañero. Enséñanos tu tarjeta terminada y recibirás un premio pequeño mientras duran existencias. El Día del Niño es celebrado en México cada 30 de abril. Es un día festivo que resalta la maravilla y alegría que los niños comparten con nosotros. El Día del Niño ha sido adaptado por la biblioteca, celebraremos el Día del Niño & Libro promoviendo la lectura y alfabetización infantil. Library staff may take photos, videos, or audio recordings at our events. Please tell us if you do not wish to be photographed, video-recorded, or audio-recorded. We will always respect your requests! An ASL (American Sign Language) interpreter or closed captioning can be provided for this program if requested at least seven (7) days in advance. For assistance, please contact your library or accessibility@aclibrary.org.

events.title: History and Historical Fiction Book Club
events.description: Join us on the 4th Wednesday of each month at 12:00 Noon for The History and Historical Fiction Book Club! This month we'll be discussing Astor: The Rise and Fall of an American Fortune by Anderson Cooper and Katherine Howe. From the publisher: "The story of the Astors is a quintessentially American story--of ambition, invention, destruction, and reinvention. From 1783, when German immigrant John Jacob Astor first arrived in the United States, until 2009, when Brooke Astor's son, Anthony Marshall, was convicted of defrauding his elderly mother, the Astor name occupied a unique place in American society. The family fortune, first made by a beaver trapping business that grew into an empire, was then amplified by holdings in Manhattan real estate. Over the ensuing generations, Astors ruled Gilded Age New York society and inserted themselves into political and cultural life, but also suffered the most famous loss on the Titanic, one of many shocking and unexpected twists in the family's story. In this unconventional, page-turning historical biography, featuring black-and-white and color photographs, #1 New York Times bestselling authors Anderson Cooper and Katherine Howe chronicle the lives of the Astors and explore what the Astor name has come to mean in America--offering a window onto the making of America itself." This program will be provided by the Davidson library staff. Information about this Group The History and Historical Fiction Book Club meets monthly to discuss Fiction and Nonfiction titles. For a list of all 2026 selections, please click here: History and Historical Fiction Book Club Reads 2026 How to Join an Online ProgramThis event takes place on Zoom. Please register by 9:00 am the morning of the event. You’ll receive an email with a secure Zoom link with a reminder about the meeting.Looking for more technology resources? Check out this list!

Indoor programs will be operating at a reduced attendance capacity and will include social distancing based on the library space. Want to be notified about future events? Subscribe to our newsletter here. events.title: Adult Book Discussion: Lady Tan's Circle of Women
events.description: Join us for a friendly conversation of Lady Tan's Circle of Women by Lisa See at our latest monthly book discussion. Copies of the book are available for checkout at the Circulation Desk.According to Confucius, "an educated woman is a worthless woman," but Tan Yunxian-born into an elite family, yet haunted by death, separations, and loneliness-is being raised by her grandparents to be of use. Her grandmother is one of only a handful of female doctors in China, and she teaches Yunxian the pillars of Chinese medicine, the Four Examinations-looking, listening, touching, and asking-something a man can never do with a female patient. From a young age, Yunxian learns about women's illnesses, many of which relate to childbearing, alongside a young midwife-in-training, Meiling. The two girls find fast friendship and a mutual purpose-despite the prohibition that a doctor should never touch blood while a midwife comes in frequent contact with it-and they vow to be forever friends, sharing in each other's joys and struggles. No mud, no lotus, they tell themselves: from adversity beauty can bloom. But when Yunxian is sent into an arranged marriage, her mother-in-law forbids her from seeing Meiling and from helping the women and girls in the household. Yunxian is to act like a proper wife-embroider bound-foot slippers, pluck instruments, recite poetry, give birth to sons, and stay forever within the walls of the family compound, the Garden of Fragrant Delights. How might a woman like Yunxian break free of these traditions, go on to treat women and girls from every level of society, and lead a life of such importance that many of her remedies are still used five centuries later? How might the power of friendship support or complicate these efforts? AccessibilityNeed sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please call (312) 747-8184 or email access@chipublib.org to request accommodations. Requests must be made at least 14 business days before the event.

events.title: Lush Lyricism and Intimate Confessions with Miranda Cowley Heller
events.description: Lush Lyricism and Intimate Confessions with Miranda Cowley Heller New York Times Bestselling Author of The Paper Palace and Reese’s Book Club PickAward-winning and bestselling author Miranda Cowley Heller takes us on an intimate journey through the life stages of a woman in her new poetry collection, What the Deep Water Knows. Delicate, yet exceedingly raw, these poems will transport you as vividly as any work of fiction. If I could fly backward, I would. To the safety of branches, to the time when my heart still raced for you, twelve hundred beats a minute. In poetry that is at once bold and lyrical, affecting and devastatingly frank, Miranda Cowley Heller takes us through childhood, marriage, motherhood, and beyond. Suffused with the natural world and the landscape of Cape Cod, where many of the poems are set, What the Deep Water Knows contemplates love in all the seasons. Register now to celebrate National Poetry Month with this graceful new poetry collection. About the Author: Miranda Cowley Heller was raised in New York City and Cape Cod. Her debut novel The Paper Palace was a number one New York Times bestseller in the US, a Sunday Times bestseller in the UK, and was longlisted for the Women’s Prize. She has worked as a senior vice president and head of drama series at HBO, developing and overseeing such shows as The Sopranos, Six Feet Under, The Wire, Deadwood, and Big Love, among others. This is her first poetry collection.

You will be asked to... Prepare program materialsTidy up program materials Providing 1-1 or small group support during an activity or craftRole model appropriate behaviour Registration Required. Want to know more about volunteering at the Library? Visit our website! As a Library volunteer, you are expected to... Be on time and reliable – Show up when you’re supposed to and follow through with your responsibilities.Be responsible and dedicated – Do your tasks well and take them seriously.Respect the staff and follow their lead – Listen to your supervisors and do what they ask.Dress and act appropriately – Wear the right clothes and behave in a professional way at all times.Keep things private – Don’t share personal information about patrons, staff, or other volunteers.

events.description: You're invited to join the Saint Paul Public Library's Next Chapter Book Club as we read Superfudge by Judy Blume. Since the fall of 2016, Saint Paul Public Library has been a Next Chapter Book Club affiliate. Next Chapter Book Club offers community-based book clubs for adolescents and adults with intellectual and developmental disabilities. Our members (including some who cannot read) love the way books transport them to different worlds where they meet interesting characters and learn about exciting new things. Taking that journey with a group of friends makes it even more delightful and fun. Join us online (click this link to connect to the virtual book club) Wednesdays 5-6 pm until we reach the end of the book. Estimated end date in mid May. Contact Erin at erin.zolotukhin-ridgway@ci.stpaul.mn.us with questions. This book will be shared online during book club.

events.title: The D.E.A.R. Community
events.description: Drop Everything and Read (D.E.A.R.) with a community that loves books and reading. Start your evening at the Wyoming Branch Library to browse and choose a book—or bring one you’re already enjoying. Then head across the street to Nourish at be. for complimentary tea and dedicated, unplugged reading time designed for rest and self-care. As we wind down, you’re invited to join a relaxed, optional conversation where we can share what we’re reading—or a book that’s deeply impacted our lives—and swap recommendations for our next great read. Whether you read quietly the whole time, join the discussion, or simply enjoy the calm company of fellow book lovers, this evening is yours. This event is geared towards independent readers that are looking for quiet time away from the hustle and bustle and enjoy reading quietly in community.

events.title: Mystery Book Club: The Long Call by Ann Cleeves
events.description: Join us on the fourth Wednesday each month to discuss a mystery book! --- This month's title is The Long Call by Ann Cleeves.In North Devon, where two rivers converge and run into the sea, Detective Matthew Venn stands outside the church as his father's funeral takes place. Once loved and cherished, the day Matthew left the strict evangelical community he grew up in, he lost his family too. Now, as he turns and walks away again, he receives a call from one of his team. A body has been found on the beach nearby: a man with a tattoo of an albatross on his neck, stabbed to death. The case calls Matthew back into the community he thought he left behind, as deadly secrets hidden at its heart are revealed, and his past and present collide.

events.title: Walk and Talk @ The Sweet Arrow Reserve
events.description: Come walk with a librarian and a naturalist in Sweet Arrow Reserve as we discuss a featured book each month all summer! -- Walk and Talk encourages book lovers to get out in nature! This collaboration between the Winters-Bellbrook Library and The Bellbrook-Sugarcreek Parks District is back with a book/walk series from April-September. This month we read Atmosphere: A Love Story, which follows Professor Joan Goodwin's journey from academia to the stars as she pursues a career with NASA in the 1980s. Walk a gentle but uneven trail which is safe for most, but remember to bring proper shoes, a water, and bug spray. Ask for your book at the reference desk at the Winters Library. Please register as we will know whom to call if the program has to move to the Parks District Headquarters on Washington Mill Road in case of bad weather. 18+

events.title: AI Unplugged: Discussion & Discovery
events.description: Curious about artificial intelligence (AI)? Love thoughtful conversations and big questions? This discussion group offers a relaxed, welcoming space to explore AI through hands-on experimentation, current news, and conversation.We’ll try out generative AI platforms, discuss how AI is being used (and misused), and unpack recent AI headlines. Together we’ll explore the risks, ethics, applications, and challenges of AI as we learn along the way. This is a shared learning space—no prior knowledge needed. Just bring your questions, curiosity, and a willingness to join the conversation about how AI is shaping our world.By participating in this program, you acknowledge that you accept and agree to Guelph Public Library's Code of Conduct. Alternative formats are available as per the Accessibility per Ontarians Act by contacting Library Communications at 519-824-6220. Accessible programs and events Sensory kits are available by request at all library programs.Please let us know a few days before a program if you need any accessibility accommodations so we can plan for you. For sign language interpreters we ask for a minimum of two weeks’ notice.When registering for a program with a support person, please include them as an additional guest.

For teens in 6th to 12th grade only. Please respect this age limit for safety and to encourage peer learning among teens. Suggested Reading: Hidden Systems

events.title: Fantasmagoria Book Club: Listen to Your Sister
events.description: Fans of horror and the supernatural are invited to join the Fantasmagoria Book Club at the Pleasant Ridge Library on Wednesday, Apr 22 from 6:45-7:45 p.m. Our April title will be Listen to your Sister by Neena Viel. Stop by the Pleasant Ridge Branch Library for a copy of the book and discussion questions. New members welcome- See you there!

events.title: Adult Book Discussion: All The Pretty Horses
events.description: Join us for a discussion of the award-winning book from the late Cormac McCarthy, All The Pretty Horses! John Grady Cole is a 16-year-old boy who leaves his Texas home when his grandfather dies. With his parents already split up and his mother working in theater out of town, there is no longer reason for him to stay. He and his friend Lacey Rawlins ride their horses south into Mexico; they are joined by another boy, the mysterious Jimmy Blevins, a 14-year-old sharpshooter.In McCarthy's unblinking imagination the boys suffer truly harrowing encounters with corrupt Mexican officials, enigmatic bandits and a desert weather that roils like an angry god. In what is perhaps the book's most spectacular feat, horses and men are joined in a philosophical union made manifest in the muscular pulse of the prose and the brute dignity of the characters.AccessibilityNeed sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please call (312) 747-8184 or email access@chipublib.org to request accommodations. Requests must be made at least 14 business days before the event.All the Pretty Horses

events.title: Dayton's Bluff Book Club
events.description: Dayton's Bluff Book Club is a great fit for folks who love reading diverse books by local authors and discussing them with a group of dedicated readers. We meet once a month on a Wednesday in the months of October, November, December, February, March, and April at 6 p.m. We have a limited supply of free books for book club participants that will be available for pick up at the Dayton's Bluff Branch Library when selections become finalized. Participants will be notified by email. Books will also be available to borrow at the library. The Dayton’s Bluff Book Club is a collaboration of the Metropolitan State University Library and the Dayton’s Bluff Branch of Saint Paul Public Library whose members live, work or attend classes on the East Side. We prioritize our selection of titles to those written by Minnesota authors, that have a local setting, or that are written by people of color and other diverse authors. Book discussions usually include issues related to social justice, equity, and anti-racism. We encourage book club members to use both the university and public libraries in pursuit of lifelong learning and the joy of reading. Discussions are frequently facilitated by authors, Metropolitan State faculty, or other guests from the community. Raised by Wolves: Fifty Poets on Fifty PoemsWelcome - Dayton's Bluff Book Club - LibGuides at Metropolitan State University

events.title: High Intermediate - Advanced ESL Book Club - Animal Farm
events.description: ESL Book Club will read Animal Farm by George Orwell. This book club is best for high intermediate - advanced English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then you will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Book club will be led by Fernando Lopez. Meeting dates: April 16, 23; May 7, 14, 21, 28. No class April 30th.Meeting time: Thursdays, 10:30am - 12:00nRegistration is open now! Book club is limited to 7 participants. Books will be provided for registered participants to keep. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting.Animal Farm:George Orwell's timeless and timely allegorical novel—a scathing satire of a downtrodden society’s blind march towards totalitarianism.“All animals are equal, but some animals are more equal than others.”A farm is taken over by its overworked, mistreated animals. With flaming idealism and stirring slogans, they set out to create a paradise of progress, justice, and equality. Thus the stage is set for one of the most telling satiric fables ever penned—a razor-edged fairy tale for grown-ups that records the evolution from revolution against tyranny to a totalitarianism just as terrible.When Animal Farm was first published, Stalinist Russia was seen as its target. Today it is devastatingly clear that wherever and whenever freedom is attacked, under whatever banner, the cutting clarity and savage comedy of George Orwell’s masterpiece have a meaning and message still ferociously fresh.

events.title: Meet the Author: Delali Adjoa
events.description: This April, witness poetry in motion with a visit from debut author Delali Adjoa, as we celebrate her young adult romance The Free Verse Society. This hate-to-love story centers on two teens who connect through their high school poetry club, where the power of the written word tears down the walls they’ve built around their hearts. Attendees who register in advance will have a chance to win a signed copy of the book! Light refreshments will also be served. For teens ages 14 and up. Presented jointly between the Dogwood Library and the Northwest Library. Raffle sponsored by the Friends of the Northwest Library. About The Free Verse Society: No one in Delray knows Jae Aƒenyo’s story—that she’s a teen mom who placed her baby for adoption—and she intends to keep it that way. After moving in with her uncle, Jae is looking for a fresh start. But an accidental run-in with the school’s delinquent, Derek Patel, is not exactly what she had in mind. She soon finds a haven in the poetry club—at least, until Derek joins.Derek Patel is desperately clinging to his old life—where his dad was alive, his mom was healthy, and they lived in an oceanfront estate instead of a run-down pink bungalow. He’ll do anything to hide his problems from his friends, including breaking into his old house to keep up the charade that he still lives there. But the house now belongs to the school’s lit teacher, who offers him the chance to join the poetry club as a penance.As the newest members of the club, Jae and Derek are tasked with planning the end-of-semester poetry reading. While Derek is hell-bent on keeping his broken family a secret, Jae is desperate to prove to her uncle that she’s more than a walking statistic—which means guarding her heart against Derek, who her uncle thinks is no good.A poignant exploration of love, loss, and the power of words to draw people together, The Free Verse Society announces the arrival of an important new voice in YA romance. Perfect for readers who love Forced Proximity, Forbidden Love, Opposites Attract, Hate to Love, the Misunderstood Bad Boy, Opposite Sides of the Tracks, and Reading/Literature Club Bonding! Questions? Reach out to youth librarians Grace Stannard (grace.stannard@fultoncountyga.gov) or Jonathan Wilson (jonathan.wilson@fultoncountyga.gov).

events.title: Inclusive Book Club with AAC (Online Event)
events.description: Hello Book Club Families, Book of the Week: The Phantom Tollbooth In The Phantom Tollbooth, Milo travels through a world where words, numbers, ideas, and imagination come alive. Along the way, he discovers that meaning can be found in unexpected places and that curiosity helps us better understand ourselves and others. This story invites us to explore language, expression, problem-solving, and the many different ways people make sense of the world About our Inclusive Book Club: Each week, an AAC user hosts the session and a librarian reads the book aloud. After the reading, we enjoy an activity or game connected to the story. We celebrate all forms of communication, including AAC, speech, gestures, and expressions. Our goal is to create a supportive and joyful space where everyone can participate in their own way. When available, we send a visual schedule 24 hours before the session to help participants follow along. Special thanks to Norton Juster and Penguin Random House for permission to share The Phantom Tollbooth as part of this program.

events.title: Adult Book Discussion: The Cliffs
events.description: Come join us for a lively discussion of The Cliffs by J. Courtney Sullivan. On a secluded bluff overlooking the ocean sits a Victorian house, lavender with gingerbread trim, a home that contains a century’s worth of secrets. By the time Jane Flanagan discovers the house as a teenager, it has long been abandoned. The place is an irresistible mystery to Jane. There are still clothes in the closets, marbles rolling across the floors, and dishes in the cupboards, even though no one has set foot there in decades. The house becomes a hideaway for Jane, a place to escape her volatile mother.Twenty years later, now a Harvard archivist, she returns home to Maine following a terrible mistake that threatens both her career and her marriage. Jane is horrified to find the Victorian is now barely recognizable. The new owner, Genevieve, a summer person from Beacon Hill, has gutted it, transforming the house into a glossy white monstrosity straight out of a shelter magazine. Strangely, Genevieve is convinced that the house is haunted—perhaps the product of something troubling Genevieve herself has done. She hires Jane to research the history of the place and the women who lived there. The story Jane uncovers—of lovers lost at sea, romantic longing, shattering loss, artistic awakening, historical artifacts stolen and sold, and the long shadow of colonialism—is even older than Maine itself.AccessibilityNeed sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please call (312) 747-8184 or email access@chipublib.org to request accommodations. Requests must be made at least 14 business days before the event.

events.title: Intermediate ESL Book Club - Number the Stars
events.description: Rianne Hastie Adams will lead an intermediate ESL Book Club for Number the Stars by Lois Lowry. This book club is best for intermediate English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then we will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Meeting dates: April 24; May 1, 8, 15, 22 Meeting time: Fridays 11:30am - 12:30pm Where: Literacy Table Registration is open now! Book club is limited to 10 participants. Books will be provided for registered participants to keep. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting. Number the Stars description:Through the eyes of ten-year-old Annemarie, we watch as the Danish Resistance smuggles almost the entire Jewish population of Denmark, nearly seven thousand people, across the sea to Sweden. The heroism of an entire nation reminds us that there was pride and human decency in the world even during a time of terror and war. A modern classic of historical fiction, Number the Stars has won generations of fans and continues to speak to today's readers.

events.description: Join our Silent Book Club at the Millbrae Library! Choose a book to read, enjoy a cup of tea, and read quietly alongside others in the library. We’ll get settled and do intros, read silently for an hour, and then share what we’re reading with others. 2:00 PM: Introductions, browse books, and quiet reading hour 3:00-3:30 PM: Share what you're reading! Light mingling optional.

events.title: Online ESL Book Club
events.description: Do you want to join a book club but feel uncomfortable with your English skills? Join our ESL Book Club! Practice reading and speaking English while having fun and making new friends. For the month of November, we will be reading American Born Chinese by Gene Luen Yang.A tour-de-force by New York Times bestselling graphic novelist Gene Yang, American Born Chinese tells the story of three apparently unrelated characters: Jin Wang, who moves to a new neighborhood with his family only to discover that he's the only Chinese-American student at his new school; the powerful Monkey King, subject of one of the oldest and greatest Chinese fables; and Chin-Kee, a personification of the ultimate negative Chinese stereotype, who is ruining his cousin Danny's life with his yearly visits. Their lives and stories come together with an unexpected twist in this action-packed modern fable. American Born Chinese is an amazing ride, all the way up to the astonishing climax.Digital and hard copies are available in the catalog. Registration is required. You will receive an email with the Zoom link for the discussion a few days before the discussion and on the day of the discussion a reminder email will also contain a Zoom link. If you are not familiar with Zoom, please review these resources: Getting Started with Zoom Zoom technical requirements For accessibility accommodations (ex. ASL), please fill out the online form at sccld.org/accessibility 7+days prior to the event. If you need assistance with the form, speak with a staff member at your local library, call (408) 540-3945, or email us at ask@lib.sccgov.org

events.title: Books and Brews: a Non-Traditional Book Club

Also we have coffee by Enderly Coffee!

events.title: Borderlands Reads Book Club
events.description: Welcome to the Borderlands Reads Book Club! This is a monthly book club with a lively discussion for adults! We are a community book club celebrating Borderlands literature featuring local and Indigenous authors, perspectives of the US-Mexico border, and stories rooted in the Sonoran Desert, inviting all to explore. We meet in person on the third Saturday of the month, from 10 to 11:30 a.m. Registration is not required. Here is the list of titles we will read in 2026 (selection and sequencing subject to availability and the whims of the club leader). There is a monthly email newsletter with reminders and updates. The book for our March meeting is The Bean Trees by Barbara Kingsolver, who just won the Pulitzer Prize and lived in Tucson. The library has very few copies, but a lot of Libby eBook versions, so attendees may need to obtain their own copy of the book (in whatever format they prefer).

events.title: Santa Clara County Bookclub Meetup
events.description: Join us for our monthly book club! We meet to discuss a selected book, alternating between fiction and non-fiction. events.title: Murphy-Wilmot Book Club
events.description: Join us to discuss what's hot in fiction and non-fiction. New members are always welcome to drop in on this informal gathering of book lovers. This month's selection is Emilia Hart's The Sirens. In 19th-century Ireland, sisters Mary and Eliza were declared convicts and shipped to Australia. As they endured horrific conditions, they held on to hope and to each other. In 2019, recurring childhood nightmares continue to afflict first-year journalism student Lucy Martin. After waking up from a traumatic sleepwalking incident, Lucy escapes to her artist sister Jess’s house in Comber Bay on the coast of New South Wales. Then Lucy discovers that her sister is missing but is reassured by a neighbor that her sister is fine and just needed a break before an important gallery exhibition. Comber Bay, known for its dark events from shipwrecks to missing men, piques Lucy’s journalistic interest, and she begins to research while waiting for Jess to return. Lucy’s dreams become more vivid as she comes closer to finding the truth of the mysteries surrounding the coastal town and her sister’s complicated past. —Library Journal Reviews

events.title: RP Mystery Group: Red Harvest
events.description: Join the Rogers Park Branch Mystery Group as we discuss Dashiell Hammett's Red Harvest."An unnamed detective arrives in Personville (nicknamed "Poisonville" by its residents) too late to help his client, who lies shot dead in an alley. It doesn't take the detective long to figure out that this murder is the least of Poisonville's problems, and he decides to stay and clean up the nasty company town on his own..." --Publisher's WeeklyCopies are available at the Reference Desk. AccessibilityNeed sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please call (312) 747-8184 or email access@chipublib.org to request accommodations. events.title: Dance Story Time with Tutu School Ballet
events.description: Join us for a special dance-inspired story time presented by Tutu School Ballet. The program includes an introduction to ballet and dance, a read-aloud from Tutu School's monthly book club selection, dance participation and more! Tutu School is a collection of whimsical, boutique ballet schools for toddlers and children across the US, UK and Canada. The school, especially their Lincoln Park studio, is passionate about helping children nurture their imagination and develop confidence and joy through music and movement. Their mission has recently extended to the Ballet Book Club, a perfect blend of dance and literacy, aimed to help young minds and bodies enjoy the world of dance through storytelling. Each month, Tutu School hand selects a different dance book for our students to dance and read along to. For kids 3 to 8 and their caregivers. How to AttendRegister by 2 p.m. the day before the event. Chicago Public Library cannot collect personal information online from kids 0 to 13. A parent or guardian’s email address must be used to register. As a reminder, school email addresses may block external emails such as those from CPL. Due to space limitations, please include the total number of children and adults as attendees when you register. AccessibilityNeed sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please call (312) 747-8184 or email access@chipublib.org to request accommodations. Requests must be made at least 14 business days before the event.

events.title: Late-Identified Neurodivergent Book Club
events.description: This book club welcomes all neurotypes. Whether you’re self-identified, officially diagnosed, questioning, or an ally, we would love to have you! The club has a special eye toward those discovering their ADHD or autism in adulthood, but we celebrate all neurodiversityThis month we will discuss The Kissing of Kissing: Poems by Hannah Emerson. We meet monthly on 4th Saturdays at the Downtown Main Library. Newcomers are always welcome! About the space and the event: Getting to the event: The Main Library is easily accessible by public transit, with the streetcar and several bus lines stopping nearby. A bike rack is available on the east side of Vine Street, between 8th and 9th Streets. Paid street parking and nearby parking lots are also available. Accessible parking can be found in the gated lot next to our North Building on the north side of 9th Street, just west of Walnut Street. To enter this lot, press the button outside the gate to speak with a staff member. Please be prepared to show your placard.Navigating the building: The library is a large, busy, urban public building. If you are sensitive to noise, you may want to bring noise-canceling headphones or earplugs. The building is wheelchair accessible, with both stairs and elevators available to reach the 3rd floor. Staff members are available to assist if you need help finding the room.Meeting location: The event will take place in the South Building, Room 3E, a spacious meeting room on the 3rd floor. The room features ample seating and natural lighting, supplemented by overhead LED lighting.Ventilation: The room is well-ventilated and equipped with air conditioning.Arrival: The staff facilitator will open the room approximately 30 minutes early. You are welcome to arrive early to check out the space, get settled, and ask any questions. The facilitator will begin with a brief introduction, invite participants to introduce themselves if they wish, and provide an overview of the session.Discussion: The book discussion will last approximately one hour.Wrap-up: After the discussion, participants are welcome to stay for socializing if they so choose. If you would like to visit the space in advance of the event or have any questions, please email michelle.thompson@chpl.org.

events.title: Noname Book Club
events.description: Noname Book Club is a Black-owned worker cooperative connecting community members both inside and outside carceral facilities with radical books. Each month, we uplift two books written by Black, indigenous, and other people of color. We believe building community through political education is crucial for our liberation. We also believe everyone (especially racialized and colonized people) should have access to unlimited educational materials. This is why we make sure all the resources we offer are free. Stay tuned for our next book club monthly selections! Get accessibility information.

events.title: Creative Collective - A Club for Teens
events.description: Are you a creative teen that enjoys working with others in a low tech no tech setting? Interested in meeting other teens with creative talents? If so, this group is perfect for you. Attendees should be interested in creative projects and be comfortable working with various materials. Projects in the teen led club will be minimally advised to allow for creative expression. The initial meeting will open with an introductory social collage project. Later the group will vote between two projects at the first meeting. Project option 1: Create Your Own Enchanted/Cursed Object. Choose from random objects provided and create a magical origin story for it. Then embellish and bind a book with your story. Or Project Option 2: No Sew Cardboard Costuming. Create a costumed look for a character (character ideas provided) with cardboard/paper and your imagination. Projects will span the length of multiple meetups depending on how the group is doing. More details at first meeting. Future projects can be decided on by participants. Joining at a later meeting? No problem. All new members get to make an introduction collage and will be fully included in the group and current project. Joining later or need more information? Please send an email to SATeen@lib.sccgov.org For accessibility accommodations (ex. ASL), please fill out the online form at sccld.org/accessibility 7+ days prior to the event. If you need assistance with the form, speak with a staff member at your local library, call (408) 540-3945, or email us at ask@lib.sccgov.org.

events.title: Adult Book Discussion: The Heaven & Earth Grocery Store
events.description: Join the Brainerd Branch for a book discussion of The Heaven & Earth Grocery Store by James McBride. In 1972, when workers in Pottstown, Pennsylvania, were digging the foundations for a new development, the last thing they expected to find was a skeleton at the bottom of a well. Who the skeleton was and how it got there were two of the long-held secrets kept by the residents of Chicken Hill, the dilapidated neighborhood where immigrant Jews and African Americans lived side by side and shared ambitions and sorrows. Books are available for pick-up at the Brainerd branch's circulation desk. Please ask staff for assistance. How to Attend This event takes place on Zoom. Register at least 48 hours before the event. Only one registration per household is needed. You’ll receive an email with a link to the secure Zoom meeting about 24 hours before the meeting. Automatic transcription is included in all CPL events using Zoom. Accessibility Need sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please call (312) 747-8184 or email access@chipublib.org to request accommodations. Requests must be made at least 14 business days before the event. events.title: Mary Ann Key Book Club Discussion: The Serviceberry
events.description: The Mary Ann Key Book Club uses reading as a catalyst for intercultural conversations around understanding and addressing past and current injustices that impact Black, Indigenous, and People of Color. Join your neighbors in a discussion of our 2026 season selection: "The Serviceberry" by Robin Wall Kimmerer. While Indigenous scientist and author Robin Wall Kimmerer harvests serviceberries alongside the birds, she considers the ethic of reciprocity that lies at the heart of the gift economy and how we can learn from Indigenous wisdom and the plant world to reimagine what we value most. Copies of the book can be reserved; eBooks and eAudiobooks are available through www.hclib.org. Sponsor: Friends of the Hennepin County Library.

No prior experience required.All materials will be provided (books, maps, miniatures, dice, paper, etc.). Recommended for teens, tweens, and young adults ages 12-24. events.title: The Rachel Ried-Athon
events.description: Haven't gotten over Heated Rivalry yet? Have we got the book club for you! Every month we'll be running a marathon through Rachel Reid's Game Changers book series - the inspiration for the hit TV show - in publication order! This month we're covering book 1: Game Changer - or, for fans of the show, episode 3! Read up on how Scott Hunter handles living as a closeted, star hockey player while starting a new relationship with cute smoothie barista Kip Grady. The Game Changers series is available at the library digitally and in limited physical copies. This is a virtual book club - registrants will receive a link to participate before the meeting.

events.title: Adult Book Discussion: Hello Beautiful
events.description: Join us for a discussion of Hello Beautiful by Ann Napolitano. William Waters grew up in a house silenced by tragedy, where his parents could hardly bear to look at him, much less love him. So, it's a relief when his skill on the basketball court earns him a scholarship to college, far away from his childhood home. He soon meets Julia Padavano, a spirited and ambitious young woman who surprises William with her appreciation of his quiet steadiness but then darkness from William's past surfaces... AccessibilityNeed sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please call (312) 747-8184 or email access@chipublib.org to request accommodations. Requests must be made at least 14 business days before the event.

events.title: Fan Art Book Club
events.description: Do you have a book you loved so much you just had to make art about it? Come to Fan Art Book Club! You can either bring in art you've done or make the fan art right in the program! Everyone will chat about their books and art and ask questions. Snacks will be provided. Ages 10 -17.

events.title: Cookbook Club: Salsa Daddy
events.description: Join the RJ Daley-Bridgeport Library as we explore various cuisines through different cookbooks. Participants should choose one recipe to prepare and share at the meeting. Attendees will gather, discuss recipes, and enjoy offerings. This month's cookbook is Salsa Daddy by Rick MartinezFrom the author of Mi Cocina comes a comprehensive (and unbelievably delicous) cookbook that focuses on one of the main pillars of Mexican cuisine: salsas. Featuring 75 salsas and 25 full meal recipes, this cookbook will have you whipping up fresh, enticing spreads in no time.This month everyone is invited to come and share salsas.AccessibilityNeed sign language interpretation or other accessibility assistance for this event? Please call (312) 747-8184 or email access@chipublib.org to request accommodations. Requests must be made at least 14 business days before the event.

events.title: Book Clubs of EPL: Truth Be Told
events.description: Love reading? Love to talk about books you've read? Come join us for lively discussions on all types of fiction and non-fiction. We'll feature different titles and authors each month. Come and share your impressions, thoughts and interpretations of a book we have all read. This month we will be reading Truth Be Told: My Journey Through Life and the Law by Beverley McLachlinFormer Chief Justice of the Supreme Court of Canada, Beverley McLachlin, recounts her remarkable life, from growing up in rural Alberta, to studying law to become a lawyer, to launching her career on the Supreme Court.Please login to your account with your library card and then register to reserve a copy of the book.

book.title: Truth Be Told: My Journey Through Life and the Law

Bring your reading homework and drop-in to read at your own pace with active support from our trained interns. Trained interns will be available to help students stay focused, pick new books to read, and help locate grade-appropriate reading resources.

events.title: High Intermediate ESL Book Club - The Murderbot Diaries Vol 1
events.description: Our high intermediate ESL Book Club will read The Murderbot Diaries Vol 1 by Martha Wells. This book club is best for high intermediate English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then we will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Book club will be led by Sheila Jurik. Meeting datesJanuary 12, 26 (No class Jan 19)February 2, 23 (No class Feb 9, 16)March 2, 9, 23, 30 (No class Mar 16)April 13, 20, 27 (No class Apr 6)Meeting time: 10:15am - 11:45amWhere: In the library - your book club leader will tell you which tables. Registration is open now! Book club is limited to 10 participants. Books will be provided for registered participants to keep. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting. The Murderbot Diaries Vol 1 description:On a distant planet, a team of scientists are conducting surface tests, shadowed by their Company-supplied ‘droid ― a self-aware SecUnit that has hacked its own governor module, and refers to itself (though never out loud) as “Murderbot.” Scornful of humans, all it really wants is to be left alone long enough to figure out who it is (and to watch its favorite show in its downtime.) But when a neighboring mission goes dark, it’s up to the scientists and Murderbot to get to the truth.

events.description: Come out to play a classic with a bookish twist- Book Bingo! Find a book to browse for chances to win prizes! Don't know where to start? No problem! We have plenty of books here!

events.title: Homeschool Adventures
events.description: Homeschooling families, discover great children’s nonfiction and fun learning activities with us. We’ll start with a nonfiction picture book read-aloud and follow with crafts, science experiments or other learning activities connected to the month’s theme. April's book is Tiny Bird: A Hummingbird’s Amazing Journey and our theme is birds and migration. While the program is formatted with homeschooling families in mind, all families with children are welcome. Please contact us if accommodations are needed.

events.title: Now Read This Book Club at Los Altos Library
events.description: This month, we will be discussing The Book of Hope: A Survival Guide for Trying Times, by Jane Goodall. The Los Altos Library Now Read This book club meets on the last Monday of the month to discuss both fiction and non-fiction books that will help us make sense of today’s world. For accessibility accommodations (ex. ASL), please fill out the online form at sccld.org/accessibility 7+days prior to the event. If you need assistance with the form, speak with a staff member at your local library, call (408) 540-3945, or email us at ask@lib.sccgov.org.

book.title: The Book of Hope: A Survival Guide for Trying Times

events.title: Teen Book Club
events.description: Read a book and we'll talk about it. Lively discussions and similar book recommendations for anyone who loved it. We meet every fourth Monday and the following week we will be showing the movie adaptation of the book we read that month! April we are reading The Wild Robot by Peter Brown.

events.title: Graphic Novel Book Club
events.description: Teens ages 12 to 18 are invited to come in for a graphic novel book discussion. Please stop in or call for monthly selections. No registration required.

events.title: Magic Tree House Book Club
events.description: Enjoy a few chapters of a Magic Tree House book, group discussion, games, and a themed activity each week. For elementary age children and their families. Registration for each session is encouraged to guarantee a spot, but walk-ins are welcome as supplies allow.

events.title: Funny Business Book Club
events.description: Get ready to giggle with these hilarious picture books. Ages 5 – 10. Registration required. --- Not your ordinary book club! Kids will listen to read alouds of some of Ms. Julie’s favorite hilarious picture books. We’ll have an activity and snack to go with it.

events.title: Silent Book Club for Tweens
events.description: Bring a book or borrow one from the library and read silently for 20 minutes. Afterwards, we will munch on some yummy snacks and share what we just read about with each other! Ages 8-12. Please only register the number of tweens participating in this activity.

events.title: Graphic Novel Club for Kids
events.description: Join our monthly graphic novel club as we read a new title each month and create our own stories based on themes from the highlighted title.Copies are available to pickup from the front desk at the Blue Ash Branch.

events.title: Mostly Fiction Book Club
events.description: New members are always welcome to our book club! Contact Ellen with any questions: 404-613-7300 or Ellen.Baxter@fultoncountyga.gov. Selections: Jan. 26 White Teeth by Zadie SmithFeb. 23 Absolution by Alice McDermottMar. 30 Bookbinder by Pip WilliamsApril 27 This Other Eden by Paul HardingMay 18 Night Watch by Jayne Anne PhillipsJune 29 Sandwich by Catherine NewmanJuly 27 Sleep by Honor JonesAug. 31 I See You've Called in Dead by John KenneySept. 28 The King's Messenger by Susanna KearsleyOct. 26 The Postcard by Anne BerestNov. 16 Heartwood by Amity GaigeDec. 14 The Correspondent by Virginia Evans

events.title: Children's Book Reading (Persian)
events.description: Join Sara Mohajeri from Kidswonder ( شگفتی های کودکانه ) for engaging Farsi book readings and building language and literacy skills in a welcoming and safe space. For kids ages 3-9.

events.title: College Hill Book Club: The Poppy Fields
events.description: Join us in discussing the novel, The Poppy Fields. The Poppy Fields is a speculative novel by Nikki Erlick, author of The Measure, about a controversial treatment center in the California desert where people sleep through grief to be healed, following four strangers and a dog on their journey there, exploring themes of loss, healing, and self-discovery with a premise reminiscent of The Wizard of Oz. The book, published in June 2025, is a story of hope and heartache, examining the lengths people will go to escape pain and the potential costs of that escape.

events.title: The Book Club @ West
events.description: Are you looking for your 'book tribe'? Try The Book Club @ West! Come and discuss a great book, experience a sense of community, enjoy a great vibe, and share lots of laughs. Together we’ll highlight Black culture and celebrate Black stories—most of our reads are by Black authors or centered on Black experiences. Lit Happens @ West! Join Us! This month's pick is House of Eve by NYT Bestseller Sadeqa JohnsonFifteen-year-old Ruby Pearsall is on track to becoming the first in her family to attend college, in spite of having a mother more interested in keeping a man than raising a daughter. But a taboo love affair threatens to pull her back down into the poverty and desperation that has been passed on to her like a birthright... Eleanor Quarles arrives in Washington, DC, with ambition and secrets. When she meets the handsome William Pride at Howard University, they fall madly in love. But William hails from one of DC's elite wealthy Black families, and his parents don't let just anyone into their fold. Eleanor hopes that a baby will make her finally feel at home in William's family and grant her the life she's been searching for.The House of Eve

events.title: English Learners Book Club
events.description: English Learners Book Club Improve your fluency, pronunciation and reading comprehension in a supportive small group environment. Native speakers and ESL students proficient in English are both welcomed. Articles, short stories, and short novels will be read. Every other Monday from 6PM-7PM. Audience: Adults. This month, join us virtually. This program will be provided by Independence Regional Library staff. For more information call 704-416-4833 or email aramos@cmlibrary.org. How to Join an Online ProgramThis event takes place on Zoom. When registering, please provide an email address so you can receive the link to connect to the virtual English Learners Book Club meeting. You’ll receive an email with a link to the secure Zoom meeting about 24 hours before the event. New to using Zoom? Check out these videos to help you get started: Zoom How-To Videos Looking for more technology resources? Check out this list! Want to be notified about future events? Subscribe to our newsletter here.

events.title: Lit Happens Book Club
events.description: Lit Happens is a book club for twenty- and thirty-somethings in the Plaza Midwood/NoDa area who love good stories, good vibes, and good coffee. We read across genres, meet monthly, and never judge anyone for choosing the audiobook. Come for the books, stay for the banter. The title for this month is Romantic Comedy by Curtis Sittenfeld. Looking for the book? Book club kit copies will be provided at the previous month's meeting or place a copy of the book on hold here! This program will be provided by Maggie at the Plaza Midwood Library. This book club will plan to meet on the last Monday of every month at the Qahwah House in Plaza Midwood at 1318 Pecan Ave in Charlotte, NC. There may be limited parking at the venue, participants are welcome to park at the Plaza Midwood library and walk. Accessibility Statement For in-person programs: Charlotte Mecklenburg Library is committed to providing accessible programs and events. This program will be offered in person at a library location. To request an accommodation, please contact us by phone or email at least one week prior to the program. Requests can be submitted by calling (704) 416-6200 or emailing malbert@cmlibrary.org.

events.title: Readability Book Group for Adults with Disabilities
events.description: The Readability Book Group is a group for adults with disabilities that is run in partnership with Center for Independent Futures. We meet weekly on Mondays via Zoom. We read a new book (or books!) every 3 months. Our sessions run from January-March, April-June, July-September and October-December. Each meeting, we will recap what we have read so far, then the facilitator will read the next part of the book aloud. Afterwards, we will discuss what we just read. Our book for April-June will be: THE BOOK WITCH by Meg Shaffer. Registration can be done here on the library website or via Center for Independent Futures. Note: There will be no book group on May 25th

events.title: Metro Year of the Book Club
events.description: 2026 is the Year of the Book at your Fulton County Library System! Join the new Metro Year of the Book Club to read all of the monthly featured titles we are recommending this year, followed by a lively discussion of the book at the end of the month. We can't wait to meet you at the library! This month's featured title is Poetry is not a Luxury. From the creator of the beloved @PoetryIsNotaLuxury Instagram account, a gorgeously wrought poetry anthology that is a gift and a guide for readers through every season of life. At our discussion, participants should feel free to bring their own favorite books of poetry or poems they have written and would like to share. Join us in celebrating poetry in all its forms!

events.description: This month’s selection: The Backyard Bird Chronicles, Amy Tan’s charming and witty collection of essays and illustrations documenting her journey into birding and nature. Co-sponsored by Audubon Everglades.

events.library: Public Library of Youngstown and Mahoning County
events.description: Join us for Morbid Manuscripts, a nonfiction book club for readers who enjoy exploring and discussing the darker side of history and human nature. This month we'll be reading Killers of the Flower Moon by David Grann. For adults 18+.

events.title: *Hybrid* Science Fiction Book Club
events.description: If you enjoy Science Fiction, then this is the book club for you! The Science Fiction Book Club meets at it's new day and time, on the 4th Monday of every month, at the Pearl Avenue Library, and on Zoom (as a hybrid meeting), starting at 4:00 p.m. Be sure to register to receive the zoom link, otherwise, we will see you at the library! Monday, April 27, 2026, 4:00 p.m. Drunk on All Your Strange New Worlds, by Eddie Robson.Eddie Robson's Drunk on All Your Strange New Words is a locked room mystery in a near future world of politics and alien diplomacy. Lydia works as translator for the Logi cultural attaché to Earth. They work well together, even if the act of translating his thoughts into English makes her somewhat wobbly on her feet. She's not the agency's best translator, but what else is she going to do? She has no qualifications, and no discernible talent in any other field. So when tragedy strikes, and Lydia finds herself at the center of an intergalactic incident, her future employment prospects look dire-that is, if she can keep herself out of jail! But Lydia soon discovers that help can appear from the most unexpected source... Monday, May 18, 2026, 4:00 p.m. Annihilation, by Jeff Vandermeer. Monday, June 22, 2026, 4:00 p.m. The Glass Bead Game, by Hermann Hesse. [audio and Link+] Monday, July 27, 2026, 4:00 p.m. The Ministry of Time, by Kaliane Bradley. Monday, August 24, 2026, 4:00 p.m. My Real Children, by Jo Walton. Monday, September 28, 2026, 4:00 p.m. The Pride of Chanur, by C.J. Cherryh. events.title: New Program: Cherryland Book Club!
events.description: Join us for our first book club discussion at the Cherryland Library! Facilitated by library staff, we will focus on well-reviewed fiction and nonfiction books for adults.For April, we will discuss They Called Us Enemy by George Takei, which is available for check out through the Library collection. In addition, a limited number of free copies of the print book will be available through Alameda County's participation in the One Book, One Coast program. Please register in advance to reserve a copy or check in the Cherryland Library after March 23rd for copies while supplies last.Synopsis:George Takei has captured hearts and minds worldwide with his captivating stage presence and outspoken commitment to equal rights. But long before he braved new frontiers in Star Trek, he woke up as a four-year-old boy to find his own birth country at war with his father's -- and their entire family forced from their home into an uncertain future. In 1942, at the order of President Franklin D. Roosevelt, every person of Japanese descent on the west coast was rounded up and shipped to one of ten 'relocation centers', hundreds or thousands of miles from home, where they would be held for years under armed guard. They Called Us Enemy is Takei's firsthand account of those years behind barbed wire, the joys and terrors of growing up under legalized racism, his mother's hard choices, his father's faith in democracy, and the way those experiences planted the seeds for his astonishing future. What is American? Who gets to decide? When the world is against you, what can one person do? --Provided by the publisher They Called Us Enemy _________ Library staff may take photos, videos, or audio recordings at our events. Please tell us if you do not wish to be photographed, video-recorded, or audio-recorded. We will always respect your requests.An ASL (American Sign Language) interpreter or closed captioning can be provided for this program if requested at least seven (7) business days in advance. For assistance, please contact your library or accessibility@aclibrary.org

events.title: Book Clubs of EPL: Know My Name
events.description: Love reading? Love to talk about books you've read? Join our book club for spirited discussions on a wide range of fiction and non-fiction. Each month we'll tackle a new title. Whether you loved it, loathed it, or left it unfinished, we want to hear your take. Come share your impressions and connect with fellow readers! This week we will be reading Know My Name: A Memoir by Chanel Miller. Universally acclaimed, rapturously reviewed, and an instant New York Times bestseller, Chanel Miller's breathtaking memoir "gives readers the privilege of knowing her not just as Emily Doe, but as Chanel Miller the writer, the artist, the survivor, the fighter." ( The Wrap ). Her story of trauma and transcendence illuminates a culture biased to protect perpetrators, indicting a criminal justice system designed to fail the most vulnerable, and, ultimately, shining with the courage required to move through suffering and live a full and beautiful life.Know My Name will forever transform the way we think about sexual assault, challenging our beliefs about what is acceptable and speaking truth to the tumultuous reality of healing. Entwining pain, resilience, and humor, this memoir will stand as a modern classic.

events.title: Reading Bingo
events.description: Join us in celebrating Día del Niño (Day of the Child) at the Union City Library throughout April. Pick up a "Reading Bingo" card at the reference desk and complete five reading activities across or diagonally on your own or with a reading friend. Show us your completed bingo card at the reference desk by Saturday, May 2, 2026, for a small prize, while supplies last. Día del Niño (Day of the Child) is celebrated in Mexico every year on April 30. It is a holiday that highlights the wonder and joy children bring into our lives. The library has adapted Día del Niño; we celebrate Día del Niño & Libro by promoting reading and children’s literacy. Acompáñanos a celebrar el Día del Niño en la Biblioteca de Union City durante todo el mes de abril. Recoge tu tarjeta de "Reading Bingo" en el mostrador de consulta y completa cinco actividades de lectura tú solo o con un compañero. Enséñanos tu tarjeta terminada y recibirás un premio pequeño mientras duran existencias. El Día del Niño es celebrado en México cada 30 de abril. Es un día festivo que resalta la maravilla y alegría que los niños comparten con nosotros. El Día del Niño ha sido adaptado por la biblioteca, celebraremos el Día del Niño & Libro promoviendo la lectura y alfabetización infantil. events.title: Art in the Afternoon: Frida Kahlo
events.description: Frida Kahlo: To Know Her Art is to Know Her! Frida Kahlo is known for her personal identity as much as she is known for her art. She has become an iconic artist and figure worldwide due to her unconventional lifestyle, marriage and political ventures that defined her life and art. While her art possesses Mexican cultural values, it is also a metaphor for the emotional and physical pain she endured. She depicted her wounded body in creative ways that often-challenged traditional female roles. If you are interested in Frida's art, then you may be amazed to learn what the works represent and why they are some of the most deeply personal and powerful artworks of the 20th century. Come ready to better understand the woman artist that is intriguing the art world today! (This Art Talk was created in collaboration with the Tucson Botanical Gardens and their new Frida’s Garden.)Presented by Kit KimballStick around afterwards to join the Tucson Art Book Club at 3:30pm.

Copies of the book are available for pickup at the front desk. In the following months, we’ll continue exploring books chosen by the group while talking about favorite characters, exciting storylines, and different perspectives. Each meeting features a lively discussion and may include a fun activity connected to the book. Register today to secure your spot!

events.title: Virtual True Crime Book Club - The Forever Witness
events.description: Love watching true crime documentaries and tv shows? Can't get enough of true crime podcasts? This is a monthly book group focused on reading and discussing True Crime. Join us on Zoom to discuss The Forever Witness: How Genetic Genealogy Solved a Cold Case Double Murder by Edward Humes.The Forever Witness "A relentless detective and a civilian genealogist solve a haunting cold case—and launch a crime-fighting revolution that tests the fragile line between justice and privacy.In November 1987, a young couple from the idyllic suburbs of Vancouver Island on an overnight trip to Seattle vanished without a trace. A week later, the bodies of Tanya Van Cuylenborg and her boyfriend Jay Cook were found in rural Washington. It was a brutal crime, and it was the perfect crime: With few clues and no witnesses in the vast and foreboding Olympic Peninsula, an international manhunt turned up empty, and the sensational case that shocked the Pacific Northwest gradually slipped from the headlines.In deep-freeze, long-term storage, biological evidence from the crime sat waiting, as Detective Jim Scharf poured over old case files looking for clues his predecessors missed. Meanwhile, 1,200 miles away in California, CeCe Moore began her lifelong fascination with genetic genealogy, a powerful forensic tool that emerged not from the crime lab, but through the wildly popular home DNA ancestry tests purchased by more than 40 million Americans. When Scharf decided to send the cold case’s decades-old DNA to Parabon NanoLabs, he hoped he would finally bring closure to the Van Cuylenborg and Cook families. He didn’t know that he and Moore would make history.Genetic genealogy, long the province of family tree hobbyists and adoptees seeking their birth families, has made headlines as a cold case solution machine, capable of exposing the darkest secrets of seemingly upstanding citizens. In the hands of a tenacious detective like Scharf, genetic genealogy has solved one baffling killing after another. But as this crime-fighting technique spreads, its sheer power has sparked a national debate: Can we use DNA to catch the murderers among us, yet still protect our last shred of privacy in the digital age—the right to the very blueprint of who we are?" -From the publisher

events.title: Intermediate ESL Book Club - Lemon Meringue Pie Murder
events.description: Carla's intermediate ESL Book Club will read Lemon Meringue Pie Murder by Joanne Fluke. This book club is best for intermediate English learners. Students will read at home each week. Then we will meet as a group once a week to talk about the story, learn new vocabulary, and practice conversation. Book club will be led by Carla Passero. Meeting dates: April 14, 21, 28; May 5, 12, 19, 26; June 2, 23, 30; July 7, 14. (No class June 9 and June 16).Meeting time: Tuesday evenings 7pm - 8pmWhere: CCC Room next to Member Services DeskLed by: Carla PasseroRegistration is open now! Book club is limited to 8 participants. Books will be provided at the first meeting for registered participants to keep, If you do not show up at the first meeting and have not made arrangements with the tutor, we will give your spot to the next learner on the wait list. Please consider your schedule and do not register if you will miss more than one meeting. About the book:Hannah Swensen thought she'd finally discovered the recipe for a perfect life. But her sometime beau Norman Rhodes tosses a surprise ingredient into the mix when he phones to tell her he's just bought a house from local drugstore clerk Rhonda Scharf--which he plans to tear down in order to build the dream home he and Hannah designed. It seems the plan has been cooking for quite some time, and Hannah's shocked. Especially since her ring finger is still very much bare. . .The good news is that the soon-to-be-torn-down house is full of antiques--and Norman has given Hannah and her mother first dibs. They uncover some gorgeous old furniture, a patchwork quilt. . .and Rhonda Scharf's dead body. A little more sleuthing turns up the half-eaten remains of a very special dinner for two--and one of The Cookie Jar's famous lemon meringue pies. Now it's up to Hannah to turn up the heat--and get busy tracking down clues. Starting in her very own kitchen. . .

events.title: Books on Tap with special guest from Pretty Gritty Tours
events.description: Join us and Chris Staudinger of Pretty Gritty Tours to discuss Plutopia, by Kate Brown.In Plutopia, Brown draws on official records and dozens of interviews to tell the stories of Richland, Washington and Ozersk, Russia--the first two cities in the world to produce plutonium. To contain secrets, American and Soviet leaders created plutopias--communities of nuclear families living in highly-subsidized, limited-access atomic cities. Brown shows that the plants' segregation of permanent and temporary workers and of nuclear and non-nuclear zones created a bubble of immunity, where dumps and accidents were glossed over and plant managers freely embezzled and polluted. In four decades, the Hanford plant near Richland and the Maiak plant near Ozersk each issued at least 200 million curies of radioactive isotopes into the surrounding environment--equaling four Chernobyls--laying waste to hundreds of square miles and contaminating rivers, fields, forests, and food supplies. This month's location will be at E9 Firehouse and Gastropub Plutopia Explore all of Tacoma Public Library's Book Clubs.

Indoor programs will be operating at a reduced attendance capacity and will include social distancing based on the library space. Want to be notified about future events? Subscribe to our newsletter here. NOTE: Charlotte Mecklenburg Library is committed to public safety and continues to intentionally and deliberately expand in-person services that align with the COVID-19 safety guidelines set by Mecklenburg County, the Mecklenburg Board of County Commissioners (BOCC), the state of North Carolina and the Centers for Disease Control (CDC). events.title: Kobetich Book Club
events.description: Join us at Kobetich Branch to discuss a new book each month. This month we will be reading The Martian by Andy Weir. We meet from 4-5pm. #1 NEW YORK TIMES BESTSELLER • NOW A MAJOR MOTION PICTURE A mission to Mars. A freak accident. One man’s struggle to survive. From the author of Project Hail Mary comes “a hugely entertaining novel that reads like a rocket ship afire” (Chicago Tribune). “Brilliant . . . a celebration of human ingenuity [and] the purest example of real-science sci-fi for many years . . . utterly compelling.”—The Wall Street Journal Six days ago, astronaut Mark Watney became one of the first people to walk on Mars. Now, he’s sure he’ll be the first person to die there. After a dust storm nearly kills him and forces his crew to evacuate while thinking him dead, Mark finds himself stranded and completely alone with no way to even signal Earth that he’s alive—and even if he could get word out, his supplies would be gone long before a rescue could arrive. Chances are, though, he won’t have time to starve to death. The damaged machinery, unforgiving environment, or plain-old “human error” are much more likely to kill him first. But Mark isn’t ready to give up yet. Drawing on his ingenuity, his engineering skills—and a relentless, dogged refusal to quit—he steadfastly confronts one seemingly insurmountable obstacle after the next. Will his resourcefulness be enough to overcome the impossible odds against him? NAMED ONE OF PASTE’S BEST NOVELS OF THE DECADE “As gripping as they come . . . You’ll be rooting for Watney the whole way, groaning at every setback and laughing at his pitchblack humor. Utterly nail-biting and memorable.”—Financial Times The book is available for check out at the Kobetich circulation desk. The Kobetich Book Club meets on the last Wednesday of the month. New members are always welcome! Please call Kobetich library staff at 253-280-2921 for assistance with locating or reserving a copy.

events.title: Better Off Read Book Club - An Unnecessary woman
events.description: Aaliya Sohbi lives alone in her Beirut apartment, surrounded by stockpiles of books. Godless, fatherless, childless, and divorced, Aaliya is her family's 'unnecessary appendage.' Every year, she translates a new favorite book into Arabic, then stows it away. The thirty-seven books that Aaliya has translated over her lifetime have never been read-- by anyone. After overhearing her neighbors, 'the three witches, ' discussing her too-white hair, Aaliya accidentally dyes her hair too blue. In this breathtaking portrait of a reclusive woman's late-life crisis, readers follow Aaliya's digressive mind as it ricochets across visions of past and present Beirut. Colorful musings on literature, philosophy, and art are invaded by memories of the Lebanese Civil War and Aaliya's own volatile past. As she tries to overcome her aging body and spontaneous emotional upwellings, Aaliya is faced with an unthinkable disaster that threatens to shatter the little life she has left.The City of Evanston is committed to promoting a Citywide culture of accessibility and inclusivity. To request an accommodation for a program, service, or activity, please call 847-866-2919 to make an ADA service request or fill out a request form online.

events.title: Mary Ann Key Book Club Discussion: The Serviceberry
events.description: The Mary Ann Key Book Club uses reading as a catalyst for intercultural conversations around understanding and addressing past and current injustices that impact Black, Indigenous, and People of Color. Join your neighbors in a discussion of our 2026 season selection: The Serviceberry by Robin Wall Kimmerer. While Indigenous scientist and author Robin Wall Kimmerer harvests serviceberries alongside the birds, she considers the ethic of reciprocity that lies at the heart of the gift economy and how we can learn from Indigenous wisdom and the plant world to reimagine what we value most. Copies of the book can be reserved; eBooks and eAudiobooks are available through www.hclib.org. Copies will also be available at Minneapolis Central Library 1st floor display and the information desk. Sponsor: Friends of the Hennepin County Library.

events.title: Other Worlds: Online Speculative Fiction Book Club
events.description: TPL'S ONLINE SPECULATIVE FICTION BOOK GROUP! Regularly meets the last Wednesday of each month via Zoom to discuss intriguing, mind-expanding books from multiple genres. Register today and join us online. April 2026 Selection: Ocean's Godori by Elaine U. ChoExciting, fresh and unique perspective science fiction from a rising Seattle writer.Ocean Yoon has never felt very Korean, even if she is descended from a long line of haenyeo, Jeju Island's beloved female divers. She doesn't like soju, constantly misses cultural references, and despite her love of the game, people still say that she doesn't play Hwatu like a Korean. Ocean's also persona non grata at the Alliance, Korea's solar system-dominating space agency, since a mission went awry and she earned a reputation for being a little too quick with her gun. When her best friend, Teo, second son of the Anand Tech empire, is framed for murdering his family, Ocean and her misfit crewmates are pushed to the forefront of a high-stakes ideological conflict. But dodging bullets and winning space chases may be the easiest part of what comes next. “Ocean’s Godori navigates the space lane blazed by Becky Chambers and James S.A. Corey, but it manages to arrive at some exciting new destinations.” — Charlie Jane Anders, Washington Post Ocean's Godori Speculative Fiction? What's that? Do you like science fiction? That's speculative fiction. Fantasy? OK, that's speculative fiction too. Horror, magical realism, alternate history, slipstream, steampunk, cyberpunk, dystopias, apocalyptic tales, and stories that are just plain weird? Yep. Those are all eligible for our speculative fiction menu!If reading outside the lines of everyday life interests you, we invite you to join our online community as we explore our reading horizons. Other Worlds might be the change of pace you're looking for.

events.title: Manga Mashup
events.description: Been reading any manga or watching any anime lately? Come share with us what you're reading and watching! We're not a regular book club. We're an "UnBook Club!" What's an Unbook Club, you ask? We don't all agree to read the same things. (After all, some like shojo more than shonen, or like the mecha genre more than the magical girls type. Some might even like both!) Here we all take turns sharing what we're currently reading, give and ask for recommendations, and share our love of manga! We play games, watch anime, make crafts, and hang out. Join us every last Thursday of the month at 4:30 p.m. in the community room. Looking for some manga to read? Check out our catalog and place a hold, or come explore our graphic novel section in the branch. Find a link to our catalog by clicking here. Information about this Group The Davidson Teen Manga Mashup meets monthly to discuss manga and graphic novel titles. This program will be provided by Davidson library staff, and is recommended for teens ages 12 to 18. A place for likeminded individuals to appreciate and explore the culture of Japan through one of its most popular forms of art and entertainment. events.title: One eRead/Un Livrel Canada Book Club and Watch Party
events.description: Help us celebrate another year of One eRead/Un Livrel Canada by joining us at the Main Library to watch the livestream of Antonio Michael Downing, host of The Next Chapter, as he interviews Éric Chacour and Pablo Strauss. There will be a book discussion following the livestream. About Éric Chacour and One eRead/Un Livrel CanadaÉric Chacour is a Canadian author who's debut novel, Ce que je sais de toi/ What I Know About You, was chosen for the One eRead/Un Livrel Canada program. One eRead/Un Livrel Canada is a a month-long, bilingual, digital book club presented by the Canadian Urban Libraries Council (CULC). The annual program promotes Canadian stories that can help Canadians grow in their understanding and appreciation of the unique aspects of their country and the lives of Canadians from coast to coast to coast.About Ce que je sais de toi/ What I Know About YouCe que je sais de toi/ What I Know About You takes readers from Cairo to Montreal with Tarek, a man bound by family expectations whose life is upended by an impossible love. A heartbreaking epic spanning fifty years, What I Know About You has been a bestseller in both English and French and has garnered accolades in North America and Europe. It was the winner of the Prix Femina Award, a finalist on Canada Reads and shortlisted for the Giller Prize and the Dublin International Literary Award to name just a few.Access Ce que je sais de toi/ What I Know About YouGuelph Public Library patrons will be able to access the English eBook and eAudiobook via CloudLibrary. The French eBook will be available via Cantook Libraries and the French eAudiobook is available as a special production by Radio-Canada’s Ohdio.By participating in this program, you acknowledge that you accept and agree to Guelph Public Library's Code of Conduct. Alternative formats are available as per the Accessibility per Ontarians Act by contacting Library Communications at 519-824-6220.

events.title: Virtual Author Talk: Jason Reynolds
events.description: Coach brings the final installment of the Track series to life by exploring the childhood of Otis “Coach” Brody, long before he became the steady, firm‑handed mentor to Ghost, Lu, Patina, and Sunny. As young Otie, he idolizes Carl Lewis and Marty McFly, lives to sprint, and treasures a pair of Jordans that make him feel unstoppable, until both the shoes and his often‑absent father suddenly disappear, forcing him to confront truths he never expected. Jason Reynolds traces how these early experiences shape the man Coach will become, revealing the vulnerabilities, dreams, and heartbreaks behind the figure who later guides the Defenders track team with such conviction and care. --- The Santa Clara County Library District is proud to present a free virtual author‑talk series featuring bestselling, award‑winning, and acclaimed writers from around the world, offering live Q&A sessions two to three times a month. Event links are sent about 24 hours in advance (or upon late registration), recordings are available for a limited time, and a full list of upcoming programs can be found here. ---For accessibility accommodations (ex.
